rule: This workspace has 3 isolated Python venvs: backend/.venv, frontend/.venv, integration-tests/.venv. Never consolidate them — dependencies conflict (e.g. pyjwt/cryptography version pins differ). Always activate the correct venv before running commands in a subdirectory.

The root Makefile orchestrates all venvs. Run `make setup` to create venvs and install ALL dependencies (5 requirements files + npm). Run `make test` for backend+frontend tests. Run `make lint` for ruff+tsc. Use this instead of manual pip install.

A pre-commit hook exists at .githooks/pre-commit and is installed via `git config core.hooksPath .githooks`. It runs in order: (1) `make format` to auto-fix formatting + `git add -u` to re-stage, (2) `make lint-backend` + `make lint-frontend`, (3) `make test-backend` + `make test-frontend`. Formatting failures can never block a commit — they are fixed automatically. SKIP_TESTS=1 skips tests only. --no-verify bypasses everything.
Backend pytest must run from within the backend/ directory (not from repo root) because tests import `from app import app` which requires backend/ to be the working directory.
Frontend pytest must run from within the frontend/ directory for the same module import reasons.
ruff is the sole Python linter and formatter. Use `ruff check .` and `ruff format .` — never autopep8, black, flake8, or isort. Config is in each project's pyproject.toml or ruff.toml.
Frontend TypeScript test files (*.test.ts) are excluded from tsconfig.json via the exclude array. They use Node's `global` which fails tsc --noEmit. Do not remove this exclusion.
frontend/.dockerignore must NOT exclude tsconfig.json — it is COPY'd in the Dockerfile for the TypeScript build stage. If tsconfig.json is added to .dockerignore, the Docker build will fail.

Integration tests use Docker/Podman Compose. Podman is configured for rootless use (subuid/subgid must be configured for the local user). Run with `COMPOSE=podman-compose make test` or just `make test` from integration-tests/.

The OIDC browser flow uses 127.0.0.1 (NOT localhost) for all URLs — redirect_uri, issuer, etc. This is critical because browsers treat 127.0.0.1 and localhost as different cookie domains. Mixing them causes auth_state/auth_nonce cookies to be invisible on the callback, breaking the anti-CSRF check.

mock_oidc.py supports MOCK_OIDC_EXTERNAL_ISSUER env var for the browser-reachable authorization_endpoint URL, while MOCK_OIDC_ISSUER is used for internal Docker-to-Docker communication (token endpoint, iss claims). Both must be set in docker-compose.yml.

The frontend Flask proxy (app.py) forwards specific cookies to the backend: the session cookie + auth_state + auth_nonce. These are defined in the FORWARDED_COOKIES set. If a new auth cookie is added, it must be added to this set or the OIDC flow will break.
SESSION_SECRET env var is required by docker-compose.yml for integration tests. Export it before running: `export SESSION_SECRET=test-secret-for-integration`.

The user's shell is fish. fish does not support bash heredoc syntax (<<). Use printf or echo with pipes instead. Example: `printf '[section]\nkey = value\n' > file.conf` instead of `cat > file.conf << 'EOF'`.
The local user does not have passwordless sudo. Avoid sudo commands in automation. If root is needed, ask the user to run it separately. Podman runs rootless — no sudo needed for containers.

GitHub Actions CI has 4 workflows: backend, frontend, integration (all PR-only + reusable via workflow_call), and publish (master push + semver tags). The publish workflow calls the 3 CI workflows as gates — images are only pushed if all CI passes. The pre-commit hook covers backend+frontend checks locally; integration tests require Docker/Podman.

This codebase is entirely AI-maintained. Pre-commit hooks should include tests (not just lint) because the cost of failing CI is paid by the human who must return and re-prompt. The AI does not mind waiting 15 seconds.
Do not create markdown summary files or documentation files unless explicitly requested. The user prefers concise, action-oriented work over documentation.

The .ai/ folder is committed to a public repo. Never write PII (usernames, hostnames, emails, real secrets, absolute paths) into .ai/lessons.jsonl. Use generic references like 'the local user' instead of actual usernames. Env var names and example/test values already present in committed code are acceptable.

The publish workflow (.github/workflows/publish.yml) calls backend, frontend, and integration CI as reusable workflow gates (needs: [backend-ci, frontend-ci, integration-ci]), then builds+pushes Docker images to GHCR on master pushes and semver tags (v*.*.*). Images are ghcr.io/<owner>/vibe-coded-backend and ghcr.io/<owner>/vibe-coded-frontend. Tags: latest (master), sha-<hash> (always), semver (on tags). Trivy scans run post-push and upload SARIF to the Security tab.

Both backend/Dockerfile and frontend/Dockerfile include OCI labels (org.opencontainers.image.title, .description, .source) and HEALTHCHECK instructions. Dynamic OCI labels (version, revision, created) are injected by docker/metadata-action in the publish workflow. Do not remove the static LABEL instructions from the Dockerfiles.
Dependabot is configured at .github/dependabot.yml to track Docker base image updates (backend + frontend) and GitHub Actions version bumps on a weekly schedule. Max 5 open PRs per ecosystem.

ALWAYS run tests and lint via the ROOT-LEVEL Makefile targets: `make test-backend`, `make test-frontend`, `make lint`, etc. from /vibe-coded. NEVER invoke python, pytest, ruff, or tsc directly — not even via a venv path. NEVER run `make test` from a subdirectory (backend/, frontend/) because those Makefiles default PYTHON=python3 (system Python, missing deps). The root Makefile is the only correct entry point because it passes the venv interpreter explicitly.

Never use MCP servers for any operation in this workspace. Use run_in_terminal with the git CLI for all git operations (commit, push, add, status, diff, log, etc.). MCP-based git tools use libgit2 which bypasses shell hooks (pre-commit, post-commit, etc.), causing hooks to silently not run.

Never bypass testing or validation, no matter how trivial the change. Never use --no-verify on git commit. Never skip lint or tests. There are no exceptions — 'doc-only', 'chore', or 'one-line fix' do not justify bypassing the pre-commit hook or CI gates.

All Makefile recipe lines (the shell commands under each target) MUST be indented with a single TAB, not spaces. CI fails with 'missing separator' if spaces are used. .PHONY and variable continuation lines (after \) may use spaces. Whenever writing or editing a Makefile, verify indentation with `grep -Pn '^ {2,}' Makefile` and confirm only continuation lines appear — not recipe lines. Run `make -n <target>` to parse-check without executing.

Be thorough. When fixing any bug or issue, immediately audit the entire codebase for the exact same problem before considering the task complete. Do not fix only the reported instance — search all relevant files for identical patterns and fix them all in the same pass.

When running the frontend locally (outside Docker), all four of these env vars are required: SERVER_HOSTNAME=localhost, DEV=true, BACKEND_HOSTNAME=127.0.0.1, BACKEND_PORT=8000. Missing BACKEND_HOSTNAME/BACKEND_PORT causes the Flask proxy to attempt DNS resolution for the Docker hostname 'backend:80', resulting in silent HTTP 500s on every proxied API call. Missing SERVER_HOSTNAME=localhost causes CSRF validation to fail due to a host mismatch between what the frontend sends and what the backend session cookie domain expects.

The profile response includes two distinct email fields: 'oidc_email' (sourced from the server-side session, always set when logged in via OIDC, never modified by user actions) and 'email' (the DB column, used only as a notification opt-in preference — null means opted out). Never conflate them. Display the OIDC provider email from 'oidc_email'; write notification preferences to 'email'. This separation was introduced to fix a regression where clearing the notification email permanently erased the user's displayed email address.

The byId() helper in pages.ts throws an error if the element is not found in the DOM. Use document.getElementById() instead for elements that are optional or conditionally present on a page (e.g., placeholder divs that only exist on one route). byId() is appropriate only for elements that are guaranteed to be present. Calling byId() on a missing element silently breaks any code that follows in the same try block.

Flask does not hot-reload HTML templates when a template file changes. After editing any file in frontend/templates/, the Flask dev server must be manually restarted to serve the updated template. Failing to restart after adding new HTML elements (e.g., a new div with an id) causes JavaScript that queries those elements to silently fail or throw, making the bug look like a JS problem when it is actually a stale-template problem.

The complete_registration endpoint now creates accounts with status='pending_approval' and clears the session. Integration tests that register via OIDC then use authenticated endpoints must: (1) close the old client after complete_registration, (2) activate the account via DB or activate_account helper, (3) re-login via oidc_login to get a fresh authenticated session. The claim-account endpoint still works with a pending_registration session and does NOT require this treatment.

When adding a new column with a SQLAlchemy default (e.g. default=SomeEnum.VALUE), also add server_default=SomeEnum.VALUE.value so that raw SQL INSERTs (commonly used in integration tests) get a proper default value. Without server_default, the column is NOT NULL with no DB-level DEFAULT, causing raw SQL INSERTs that omit the column to fail. Also update the Alembic migration to include the column with server_default.

After db.commit() in a SQLAlchemy session context, ORM objects become detached. Accessing lazy-loaded attributes (like act.id) after commit raises DetachedInstanceError. Always capture needed attribute values (e.g. act_id = act.id) BEFORE committing or leaving the session context, especially if the response references those values.

Read .ai/lessons.jsonl at the START of every conversation turn — not just for code changes but for ALL operations including git push, git status, file reads, etc. The lessons apply to every action taken in this workspace, not only code edits. Skipping the lesson-reading step for 'trivial' actions is how rules get violated.

After a successful mutation (API POST/PATCH/DELETE), always re-fetch and re-render the affected UI sections. Never show only a status message — the user expects the data to update immediately. Extract rendering into helper functions that can be called both on initial load and after mutations.

When running the backend locally with the mock OIDC provider, set TEST_REDIRECT_URI to point at the frontend port (e.g. http://127.0.0.1:5000/api/v2/auth/callback/test) not the backend port. This ensures the OIDC callback goes through the frontend proxy, keeping the entire auth flow on a single origin so relative redirects (like /auth/complete-registration) land on the frontend, not the backend (which would 404).

Do not create separate pages for the same feature that differ only by user privilege level. Use a single page and conditionally show management sections based on the user's claims. Separate pages lead to duplicated UI, confusing navigation, and drift between the read-only and management views.

Always use `make dev` and `make dev-stop` to start and stop the local development environment. Never start backend, frontend, or mock OIDC servers manually — the Makefile centralises all env vars, port assignments, and process management. This avoids forgotten env vars, wrong ports, and orphaned processes.

OIDC provider support is enum-driven. To add a new provider: (1) add a member to ExternalAuthProvider in models/enums.py with (auto(), 'https://provider/.well-known/openid-configuration-base'), (2) set env vars {PREFIX}_CLIENT_ID, {PREFIX}_CLIENT_SECRET, {PREFIX}_REDIRECT_URI, (3) add a login/register link in the frontend pages.ts renderLogin(). The auth router auto-creates handlers for every enum member at startup — no other backend changes needed.

The `get_accounts_with_claim` function filters by `AccountStatus.ACTIVE`. Test accounts that need to be found by claim-based queries must have `act.status = AccountStatus.ACTIVE` set before committing. The default status is `PENDING_APPROVAL` which excludes them from claim queries.

Defunct (disabled) accounts are allowed read-only access through RequireLogin. All mutation endpoints must call `require_write_access(account)` to enforce the read-only restriction. The account's existing data (mealbot records, happy hour history) is preserved — only new writes are blocked.

CSS themes use custom properties (--bg-body, --fg-accent, etc.) defined in :root and overridden via [data-theme='xxx'] selectors in themes.css. The theme is applied instantly from localStorage on page load (before JS runs) via an inline script in base.html, and synced to the server via PUT /api/v2/account/theme.

In production, OIDC anti-CSRF cookies use the __Host- prefix (e.g. __Host-auth_state) to prevent subdomain override attacks. The authenticate endpoint reads cookies by the handler's configured key name (which includes the prefix in prod). The AuthenticateCookies Pydantic model was removed — cookies are now read directly from request.cookies.
SESSION_SECRET must be at least 32 characters in production. The config validator enforces this at startup. Test suites that monkeypatch production mode must use a secret of 32+ characters.

HAPPY_HOUR_TYRANT is intentionally self-assignable by any authenticated user via PATCH /api/v2/account/claims. This is by design — users opt in to the tyrant rotation voluntarily. Do NOT add it to BLOCKED_CLAIMS. Only ADMIN and BASIC are blocked from self-service.

Disaster recovery endpoints exist for both Happy Hour and Mealbot: PATCH/DELETE /api/v2/happyhour/events/{id} (update/cancel events), POST /api/v2/happyhour/rotation/skip (voluntary turn skip), DELETE /api/v2/mealbot/record/{id} (void receipts). Event cancel frees the week_of unique constraint so a replacement event can be created. SKIPPED rotation status does not count toward the 3-consecutive-miss limit (unlike MISSED).

TyrantAssignmentStatus has 5 values: SCHEDULED, PENDING, CHOSEN, MISSED, SKIPPED. The SKIPPED status is for voluntary turn skips and is intentionally excluded from get_consecutive_misses() counting — it neither increases the miss count nor breaks a miss streak.

All disaster recovery endpoints accept ADMIN as an alternative to feature-specific claims. Event update/cancel and rotation skip require HAPPY_HOUR_TYRANT OR ADMIN. Mealbot void allows ADMIN to void any record regardless of involvement. ADMIN users can also skip anyone's rotation turn (not just their own). The route gate uses a lower claim (HAPPY_HOUR or MEALBOT) and the TYRANT-or-ADMIN check is done inside the handler body.
